bibliographic.citation: Die Winteney-Version der Regula S. Benedicti: Lateinisch und Englisch, mit Einleitung, Anmerkungen, Glossar und einem Facsimile zum erstenmale herausgegeben, ed. Arnold Schröer (Halle: Max Niemeyer, 1888).
bibliographic.title: Die Winteney-Version der Regula S. Benedicti: Lateinisch und Englisch, mit Einleitung, Anmerkungen, Glossar und einem Facsimile zum erstenmale herausgegeben

entities.description: The first printed edition, presenting Latin and English on alternating pages with an introduction, notes, glossary, and a drawn facsimile plate of the English manuscript incipit.
entities.label: Arnold Schröer's 1888 edition of the Winteney Rule
notes: The English verbal text occupies printed pp. 3, 5, 7, 9 and odd pp. 13-147; the drawn facsimile plate is BSB canvas 209.
The DDB record describes the digitized exemplar as a Lumoprint copy.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1888. Current Copyright Office guidance places publications before 1 January 1931 in the U.S. public domain as of 2026, subject to the guidance's stated restoration caveat; a maximum 95-year publication term expired no later than 1983.
rights.component: 1888 published edition text, editorial matter, typography, and plate
notes: The conclusion rests on publication date, not on an unreconciled namesake lifespan.
It does not determine provider-imposed conditions on BSB or U-M reproductions of the edition.

entities.label: BSB complete digital facsimile of Schröer's 1888 edition

entities.description: A composite parchment codex containing the bilingual Rule at ff. 52r-140r, with Latin and English passages alternating. The target English adaptation begins at current f. 54r and ends at f. 140r; adjacent codex contents are distinct works.

holding.notes: The codex was owned or used by Wintney Priory from the thirteenth through fifteenth centuries; this provenance does not by itself establish production there.

identifiers.scheme: British Library Archives and Manuscripts record

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Claudius D III
notes: The manuscript's obsolete a/b foliation is one leaf behind current foliation in this section: Schröer's old 52a corresponds to current f. 54r, and old 138a to current f. 140r.
The reviewed catalog assigns Protogothic and Gothic hands across the codex; the relevant Rule hand is separately dated by EM1060.

rights.basis: Physical ownership, reading-room access, the medieval verbal work, and copyright or provider terms in digital reproductions are distinct layers.

dateStatements.notes: The evidence permits multiple revision stages and does not identify a named adapter.
dateStatements.value: by the first quarter of the thirteenth century

entities.description: The anonymous English prose adaptation and revision of Æthelwold's Old English Rule made for female religious readers, structured as a prologue and chapters I-LXXIII and uniquely preserved in Cotton MS Claudius D III.
entities.label: The Wintney Version of the Rule of St Benedict
notes: Middle English is used as a discovery classification with medium confidence. The text is essentially based on an Old English translation, and scholarship warns that a rigid Old/Middle English division obscures its layered revision.

rights.basis: The anonymous early-thirteenth-century work was fully published in Schröer's 1888 edition. Current Copyright Office guidance says works published before 1 January 1931 are in the U.S. public domain as of 2026, subject to its stated restoration caveat; even a maximum 95-year publication term would have expired no later than 1983.
rights.component: Medieval English verbal work as published in 1888
notes: This is a publication-based analysis and does not depend on identifying or calculating from any editor's lifespan.
This cataloging conclusion is not legal advice and does not determine rights in modern transcriptions, annotations, scans, or provider databases.

rights.basis: The work is anonymous and early thirteenth century, and the exact English text was fully published in 1888. The reviewed IPO guidance's special 2039 term concerns certain pre-1989 unpublished works, so it appears inapplicable to this published text layer.
rights.component: Medieval English verbal work published in 1888
notes: This cataloging conclusion is not legal advice. It is intentionally separate from British Library image rights and provider terms.

entities.description: The Old English Benedictine Rule tradition attributed to Æthelwold that underlies the Wintney adaptation. This abstract work layer does not identify a particular surviving manuscript as the physical exemplar used by the Wintney reviser or revisers.

rights.basis: The source work is medieval and necessarily predates the Wintney adaptation and its 1888 publication.

rights.basis: The ancient Latin source work predates both medieval English versions and modern copyright terms.

next_steps: Consult Gretsch's 1978 Anglia article and Jayatilaka's full textual study.
Keep work-level agreement with Æthelwold's tradition separate from identification of a physical exemplar.
Do not identify the exact facing Latin text in Cotton Claudius D III as the reviser's exemplar; reviewed scholarship says the reviser and compiler used different Latin texts.
openQuestions.question: How many historical revision stages produced the Wintney adaptation, and can any surviving Old English or Latin witness be identified as a direct physical exemplar?

next_steps: Review palaeographical, codicological, and institutional scholarship on the manuscript's production.
Keep Wintney as association and provenance until direct production evidence is found.
openQuestions.question: Were the English adaptation and surviving Cotton copy produced at Wintney Priory, or does the current evidence establish only later use and ownership there?

next_steps: Retry the stable resolver and item interface without automated-client blocking.
Ask U-M Digital Collections for a current IIIF or image-service inventory if exact historical-image access becomes necessary.
Continue using the independently verified complete BSB edition facsimile for reproducible page inspection.
openQuestions.question: Do all 206 historical U-M TIFF page images remain publicly retrievable through current item-specific endpoints?

next_steps: Search institutional catalogs and publisher successors for licensed digital access.
Inspect a library copy if the Gretsch supplement becomes necessary for a later revision-history decision.
Retain Hargreaves's review as indirect evidence and do not infer details beyond it in the meantime.
openQuestions.question: Can an authorized or otherwise lawful exact digital copy of the 1978 reprint and Gretsch supplement be located for direct inspection?

relations.subjectLabel: H. Hargreaves's 1980 review of the Schröer-Gretsch edition

reviewNotes: The target is one English prose adaptation comprising a prologue and chapters I-LXXIII. The XML's two chapter-seven DIV2 units, numbered 7 and 7b, are an encoding/editorial split and do not create a seventy-fourth chapter.

The manuscript target runs from the English incipit at current f. 54r through the English explicit at f. 140r. Latin and English passages alternate within that range, so canvases 117-289 are target-bearing but not English-only.
Wintney is modeled as association and provenance. The reviewed evidence does not establish that either the adaptation or the surviving copy was produced there.
Artamonova cautions that the reviser and the Cotton compiler used different Latin texts. The facing Latin in the surviving manuscript is therefore not asserted as the reviser's exact exemplar.

The BSB edition scan is a complete facsimile of the 1888 volume. Its canvas 209 reproduces only the manuscript's English incipit leaf through Schröer's drawn plate; it is a partial, second-generation witness reproduction, not a whole-manuscript facsimile.

The 1978 publication is collateral: it exactly reprints Schröer's Latin-English text and adds a new Gretsch supplement. No public exact copy was verified, and rights in the reprinted 1888 layer are not conflated with rights in the new 1978 matter.
Public access to a source or a local research cache does not itself establish permission to redistribute provider images or modern scholarship.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a pinned copy of University of Michigan item AGV8488.0001.001, an uncorrected OCR encoding keyed selectively from a 206-image carrier of Arnold Schröer's 1888 bilingual edition. Schröer transcribed the alternating Latin and English Rule from the unique Cotton MS Claudius D III witness. The cataloged work is the English adaptation at current ff. 54r-140r, not the manuscript's alternating Latin text, the whole codex, the printed edition, or its digital carriers. Scholarship identifies it as a first-quarter-thirteenth-century adaptation and revision of Æthelwold's Old English Rule; that Rule in turn is a version of Benedict's Latin Rule. No exact surviving English or Latin physical exemplar is asserted. Complete BSB edition and British Library manuscript IIIF bundles, Artamonova's article, current U.S. Copyright Office term guidance, and the two reconciled GND authority records are preserved as six ignored research caches with exact checksums; access, copyright, provider conditions, and redistribution permission remain separate assertions.

catalogSummary.title: The Wintney Version of the Rule of St Benedict

catalogingSubject.label: The Wintney Version of the Rule of St Benedict
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous English prose adaptation and revision of Æthelwold's Old English Rule of St Benedict made for female religious readers and preserved uniquely in British Library Cotton MS Claudius D III. Its intellectual structure is one prologue followed by chapters I-LXXIII. The cataloged work is not the alternating Latin text in the manuscript, the whole trilingual codex, Arnold Schröer's 1888 bilingual edition, the 1978 reprint and Mechthild Gretsch supplement, the University of Michigan image carrier or digital text, or the checked-in XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Prologue

notes: The XML's separate DIV2 N='7b' continues chapter VII's twelve degrees of humility and is an encoding/editorial split, not an independently numbered medieval chapter.
contentParts.summary: Seventy-three chapters governing doctrine, offices, worship, discipline, material life, labor, hospitality, formation, hierarchy, and mutual charity in a female religious community.

assertion.notes: The surviving early-thirteenth-century copy supplies a terminus ante quem, not proof that every adaptation stage occurred when or where that copy was made.

dateStatements.display: By the first quarter of the thirteenth century; possibly through more than one earlier revision stage

assertion.notes: This dates the unique witness and first known attestation, not a securely isolated original adaptation event.

dateStatements.display: Early thirteenth century, probably soon after 1200

extent.description: One prose adaptation with one prologue and seventy-three chapters. The target Middle English DIV1 in the repository XML contains 75 DIV2 containers because chapter VII is split into N='7' and N='7b'; that encoding split does not create a seventy-fourth chapter. The same DIV1 contains 187 P, 74 PB, 231 MILESTONE, 504 NOTE1, 15 DIV3, and no L or LG elements. Its 74 PB include an omitted-front-matter placeholder and a German edition heading in addition to 72 printed English text pages; the complete file's 206 PB serialize the edition's non-linear image order and are not 206 target pages.

formStatements.description: One continuous prose rule organized as a prologue and seventy-three chapters; the repository target DIV1 contains no L or LG verse elements.

assertion.notes: The work adapts an earlier vernacular translation rather than freshly translating the exact facing Latin text in Cotton Claudius D III.

Middle English is retained as the single discovery facet because the U-M corpus catalogs the text that way and the copy is early thirteenth century. The British Library catalogs the same language as Old English; Artamonova explains that the text remains essentially Æthelwold's Old English translation while its spelling, inflection, and some passages were updated. Adding a second ang facet would misleadingly make one transitional English text appear bilingual.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: This periodization choice does not erase the text's Old English linguistic and textual base. The alternating Latin chapters belong to a separate work and are outside this work-language facet.

Schröer's a/b folio milestones preserve a superseded foliation one leaf behind the modern manuscript foliation and begin the English prologue at old folio 52a/current f. 54r; retain this crosswalk instead of silently relabeling the XML milestones.
Printed page 11 and BSB canvas 43 contain the continuation of the Latin chapter list, not target English prose; English chapter I begins on printed page 13/BSB canvas 45.

The complete BSB bundle is a facsimile of the 1888 edition carrier and the complete BL bundle is a facsimile of the manuscript carrier. Complete capture of either does not collapse edition, witness, or intellectual work layers.

next_steps: Consult Gretsch's 1978 Anglia article and Jayatilaka's full textual study.
Distinguish work-level agreement with Æthelwold's tradition from evidence of a specific physical exemplar.
Do not identify the exact facing Latin text in Cotton Claudius D III as the reviser's exemplar; current scholarship says the reviser and compiler used different Latin texts.
openQuestions.question: How many historical revision stages produced the Wintney adaptation, and can any surviving Old English or Latin witness be identified as a direct physical exemplar?

next_steps: Review palaeographical, codicological, and institutional scholarship on the manuscript's production.
Keep Wintney as an association and provenance until direct production evidence is found.
openQuestions.question: Were the English adaptation and the surviving Cotton copy made at Wintney Priory, or does the evidence establish only later use and ownership there?

next_steps: Retry the stable resolver and item interface without automated-client blocking.
Ask U-M Digital Collections for a current IIIF or image-service inventory if needed.
Continue using the independently verified complete BSB edition facsimile for reproducible page inspection.
openQuestions.question: Do all 206 historical U-M TIFF page images remain publicly retrievable through current item-specific endpoints?

assertion.notes: The English vernacular tradition and English witness support a broad cultural origin only.

The current British Library catalog confirms Wintney ownership from the thirteenth to fifteenth centuries, while EM1060 gives the manuscript's origin as unknown. Association must not be promoted to a proven place of adaptation or copying.

assertion.notes: Artamonova allows multiple revision layers; this record therefore does not collapse the history into one securely dated act by one person.

notes: This is a transmission-layer responsibility and does not make Benedict or Schröer the author of the Wintney adaptation.

summaries.value: An anonymous English adaptation of Æthelwold's Old English Rule of St Benedict for a female religious community. A prologue frames obedience and conversion as the way to eternal life; seventy-three chapters regulate kinds of religious, the abbess, counsel, humility, worship, discipline, property, food and drink, work and reading, hospitality, admission, offices, travel, mutual obedience, and communal charity. The work remains textually and syntactically close to its Old English base while updating forms and rewriting some passages.

The U-M encoding includes the 1888 title page and a German section heading, keys only the English prologue and chapters from the bilingual edition, and retains page-break placeholders for omitted front matter, Latin pages, and back matter. Those carrier and editorial layers are not parts of the medieval work.

The Old English versus Middle English label is a real scholarly periodization problem, not evidence of two languages in the target work. This record uses Middle English for discovery while preserving the British Library's Old English label and Artamonova's account of an early-thirteenth-century revision that remains essentially Æthelwold's Old English translation.

assertion.notes: A normalized discovery title using the British Library's current spelling Wintney.

titles.value: The Wintney Version of the Rule of St Benedict

notes: Winteney is the conventional spelling in Schröer's edition and much subsequent scholarship.
titles.value: The Winteney Version of the Rule of St Benedict

assertion.notes: The spelling follows the encoded Schröer text; EM1060's direct witness transcription differs slightly.
